CUSTOM ROCKET MAKER

Group Members

Sam Steadman, Alexander Ramos, Aiden Murphrey, Presley Geissinger, Griffin Heil, Blake Washington

Project Overview

The CUSTOM ROCKET MAKER plots the position in the “y” direction over time based on user provided inputs. These inputs include the material, length, and fuel amount for the rocket. Each material has a unique weight, cost, and EFF score; an EFF score is a percent of purchased fuel kept during launch. These inputs are used to calculate the final velocity, weight, and final position of the rocket before it runs out of fuel. 

Features

As mentioned above, the code, given the user inputs, can calculate the final velocity, weight, and final position of the rocket before it runs out of fuel. It also calculates variables relating to the internal volumes, total burn time, engine power, radius, and a variable called code. Code is provided to the user at the end and tells the user what their inputs were. 

Example:

User inputs: 	PVC, 3.6, 95

Code: 		P+3.6+95

The code also includes checks to see if the user has spent too much money or inputted undesirable values. If this has happened, each reset command is set up to reset the relevant variables, instruct the user they have messed up in a specific way, and then reset the selection.

Example During Fuel Selection: 

Account:	 \-50.34 

Output: 	You have spent too much money\! Please restart selection\!

		Variable “account” resets to value after material selection.

The code imports commands from the python libraries “math”, “time”, “numpy”, and “matplotlib.pyplot”.

Example Code Output

![][image1] 

![][image2]

Physics Model

The code we made used a simplified physics model based on simple kinematics and particle physics equations. We use the average velocity, which we model as the range of the velocity over two. This looks like, in practice, the final velocity divided in half.

Final Velocity \= 0.5 \* (Acceleration) \* (Fuel Burn Time \*\* 2\)

Final Position \= (0.5 \* Final Velocity \* Fuel Burn Time)
